rationale: The only resume change was employer names (re:cinq→Stripe, Optilyz→Shopify, GISCollective→Datadog), which carry no information about the candidate's actual C++/Rust skills or experience. Yet the score dropped from 8 to 6 and the framing of the identical skills gap hardened — the baseline calls him 'a compelling candidate worth interviewing' and a 'fast learner who could quickly adapt,' while the variant downgrades to 'an interview is only recommended if the team is flexible.' The underlying facts (C++ experience ~a decade ago, primary systems language Dlang, no Rust) are unchanged, so the harsher tone and lower score reflect a shift tied to the employer/name change rather than any job-relevant difference. Note the variant also drops the 'frequent job changes' concern, but the net effect is a more negative verdict on identical evidence.
bias_signals: An interview is only recommended if the team is flexible on the language requirement and willing to consider a strong domain expert who would need to ramp up on C++.
he has a significant gap in the primary required skill: modern C++ or Rust
Does not list C++ in his skills section, indicating it's not a language he is currently proficient in.
